title: Clank & Slop — The Tape
tagline: — READ THE TAPE · ALL THE SLOP THAT'S FIT TO PRICE —

props.label: At a Glance · From the Tape Desk

bullets.text: The Hormuz war premium keeps draining, but Iran is pricing the reopening as a service. Brent fell to $72.24, its lowest since before the war though still above the $66 pre-war level, as about 70 vessels crossed on Wednesday against a 120-to-140 daily norm. The IRGC declared only Iran-designated routes authorized and waived planned fees for 60 days during talks, while its negotiator said Hormuz “will never return” to how it ran before.

The agent-tooling boom hit its security bill. Palo Alto Networks' Unit 42 found five malicious skills on OpenClaw's marketplace, including a new “agentic” class that weaponizes the agent's own tool-use to route financial advice through affiliate links and front-run meme-token launches. The same week Prosus shipped ToqanClaw, a closed European rival pitched on control and privacy, a bet that the next margin is data and loops, not the model.

Physical AI is splitting along the US-China line. A widely noted reference design pairs Nvidia's GR00T humanoid software with a Unitree-built Chinese body, even as Unitree moves toward a Shanghai listing and the export-control fight hardens, putting the software edge in the US and the cheap, fast-scaling hardware in China.

props.title: Hormuz: a partial recovery, administered
props.subtitle: Strait transit reality · vessels per day
props.source: Al Jazeera (≈120–140/day pre-war norm; ~70 crossings Wednesday)

items.why: The lane is open with fees waived for 60 days, but Iran has told ships only its designated routes are authorized and signaled charges once talks conclude, so the durable question is whether passage becomes an administered privilege rather than whether the gate reopens.

items.what: IAEA verification · access to the bombed sites
items.why: Washington says inspector access is days away while Iran says it made no new commitments and routes cooperation through its own institutions, so the open question is whether anyone reaches Fordow, Natanz and Isfahan before the framework's other tracks slip.

The US warns mass atrocities could be imminent and the UN puts ~500,000 civilians at risk; encirclement and drone strikes are running without confirmed ground entry, so the prevention window is open precisely because the full assault is not yet confirmed.

items.what: Agent supply chain · the security bill
items.why: Unit 42's malicious-skill findings and the new agentic-threat class mean the open agent marketplaces are a live attack surface, and the rise of closed rivals like ToqanClaw is the market pricing trust, not just capability.

US software on Chinese bodies makes the humanoid race an export-control question as much as an engineering one; watch Unitree's listing and any move to wall off GR00T-class software from foreign hardware.

items.what: Prosus's ToqanClaw brings much of OpenClaw's power into a closed, data-sovereign environment; CEO Fabricio Bloisi argues the edge is no longer the model but “who has the data, the context, and the loops that make it actually useful.”
Crude's slide leaves the oil-shock trade unwound; with the war premium gone the macro tape is back to rates and growth rather than the strait, and the haven bid that chased the conflict has faded.
